sections.heading: S
sections.section_value: The patient reports elbow pain on the inside of their elbow, exacerbated by pottery creation. He reports pain radiating down his arm and along the inside of his arm. He denies any prior elbow injuries. The pain is rated as 7/10 and keeps him up at night. He has been taking ibuprofen for the pain but reports it's not fully effective. He has tried ice initially but it hasn't provided sustained relief. The pain has been present for approximately four days. He occasionally forgets to take his medications, particularly the metformin, due to being busy. He admits to a sweet tooth and enjoys baking cakes and cinnamon rolls, often using fondant and occasionally adding protein to balance the sweetness. He reports having a diabetic consult a long time ago and would appreciate a refresher on managing his diet.

sections.heading: O
sections.section_value: Blood pressure is 120/70 mmHg. Pulse rate is 72 bpm, regular. Respiratory rate is 18 breaths per minute, unlabored. Oxygen saturation is 100% on room air. Physical exam reveals moderate tenderness to palpation at the medial epicondyle of the right elbow with internal rotation. Resistance to pronation of the right forearm elicits pain. Flexion of the wrist elicits pain. No bruising or swelling is noted. X-ray of the right elbow reveals no fractures or bony abnormalities. Neurological exam is nonfocal. Lung sounds are clear bilaterally with no rales or wheezes. The patient's vital signs are within normal limits.

sections.heading: A
sections.section_value: The primary problem is medial epicondylitis, evidenced by the patient's elbow pain and tenderness. The patient's hypertension is well-controlled with lisinopril. The patient's diabetes is being managed with metformin, but requires closer monitoring and potential adjustments. A referral to diabetic counseling is recommended to assist with dietary management and adherence.

sections.heading: P
sections.section_value: Order a sling for the patient's right arm to provide support and limit movement. Prescribe 600 mg ibuprofen every 6 hours with food for 7 days. Order an A1C test to assess long-term glycemic control. Increase metformin dosage to 1000 mg twice daily. Schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to reassess symptoms and adjust treatment plan. Refer the patient to a certified diabetes educator for comprehensive dietary counseling and support. Continue lisinopril at 10 mg daily. Schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to monitor blood pressure and medication adherence.
